services.name: OpenAI ChatGPT (consumer/Team/Enterprise web + apps)
services.notes: Primary egress is browser paste/upload into chatgpt.com (now the canonical host; chat.openai.com redirects). Desktop/mobile apps hit the same web surface plus *.chat.openai.com. File uploads land on files.oaiusercontent.com. Voice uses UDP/3478 (WebRTC). All TLS - a non-MITM filter sees only SNI/host, not the prompt body. Default unsanctioned because the consumer tier may train on data unless org has a ChatGPT Enterprise/Team contract; promote to sanctioned only for the licensed tenant.

services.name: OpenAI API (platform)
services.notes: Programmatic egress from code, IDE assistants, SDKs and agents. All traffic to api.openai.com over TLS 443; body is JSON chat/completions/embeddings/files payloads, invisible without MITM. Bearer key in Authorization header (also encrypted). Azure-hosted variant uses <resource>.openai.azure.com and cognitiveservices.azure.com - treat separately. Monitored rather than blocked because API use is often legitimate dev/prod traffic; egress risk is bulk data sent to embeddings/files endpoints.

services.name: Anthropic Claude (claude.ai web/app)
services.notes: Consumer/Pro web egress is browser paste/upload into claude.ai; the desktop app and Claude Code also authenticate via claude.ai. File/artifact uploads may transit storage.googleapis.com (shared Google CDN - cannot be blocked wholesale without collateral). TLS-only; filter sees SNI claude.ai but not prompt content. Default unsanctioned for the consumer surface; promote to sanctioned for licensed Team/Enterprise tenants.

services.name: Anthropic Claude API (platform / Claude Code)
services.notes: Programmatic Messages API egress over TLS 443 to api.anthropic.com (a-api.anthropic.com is the anycast variant). Used by SDKs, IDE plugins, Claude Code and agent frameworks. x-api-key header is encrypted. Body is JSON message content - not visible without TLS interception. Monitored because legitimate dev traffic dominates; watch for large file/document blocks in request bodies.

services.name: Google Gemini (app + AI Studio)
services.notes: Consumer egress via paste into gemini.google.com (formerly bard.google.com); developer egress via aistudio.google.com (proxies to generativelanguage.googleapis.com) and Vertex AI (*-aiplatform.googleapis.com). Hard to fully block by host because *.googleapis.com and *.google.com are shared with Workspace/Search - blocking them breaks the org. SNI-based selective allow is fragile. Default monitored; if the org runs Workspace with Gemini governance, treat gemini.google.com as sanctioned within tenant.

services.name: Microsoft Copilot (M365 Copilot + consumer Copilot)
services.notes: We deliberately list ONLY the Copilot-distinct surfaces: consumer copilot.microsoft.com (formerly Bing Chat / edgeservices.bing.com) and the M365 Copilot subdomain copilot.cloud.microsoft. The shared M365 substrate hosts (office.com, outlook.office.com, m365.cloud.microsoft, substrate.office.com, bing.com) are intentionally NOT listed: DestinationClassifier uses suffix matching, so including them would classify ordinary Office/Outlook mailbox sync and Bing web search as Copilot and flood network-audit.jsonl / the SIEM with false AI-use events for any tenant that simply uses Microsoft 365. Enterprise M365 Copilot genuinely rides inside that shared substrate over WSS, so it is indistinguishable from normal Office traffic at the host level - separating it requires payload inspection or an explicit per-tenant M365-Copilot setting, not host classification. Monitored overall; consumer copilot.microsoft.com leans unsanctioned.

services.name: GitHub Copilot (IDE code completion + Chat)
services.notes: Egress is source code: editor buffers/context sent for completions to *.githubcopilot.com and Chat to api.githubcopilot.com (Chat moved off copilot-proxy.githubusercontent.com in 2024). TLS-only; the code payload is invisible without MITM. *.githubusercontent.com and github.com are shared with normal Git/raw-content use, complicating host-only blocking. Monitored; the data-leak concern is proprietary code leaving via IDE rather than browser paste.

services.name: Perplexity AI
services.notes: Browser-paste and query egress to perplexity.ai; developer/agent egress to api.perplexity.ai (Sonar models) over TLS 443. Comparatively clean, dedicated hostnames make host/SNI-based detection reliable. Default unsanctioned consumer research tool; uploaded files/documents are the egress risk.

services.name: Mistral AI (Le Chat + API)
services.notes: Consumer egress via paste into chat.mistral.ai (Le Chat); developer egress to api.mistral.ai and the code-specific codestral.mistral.ai. Dedicated hostnames make SNI detection reliable. TLS-only payloads. Default unsanctioned for Le Chat; api.mistral.ai is monitored dev traffic.

services.name: Cohere
services.notes: Primarily an enterprise API/RAG vendor; egress is programmatic to api.cohere.com (legacy api.cohere.ai) - chat, embed, rerank, and document ingestion endpoints. coral.cohere.com is the chat UI. Embeddings/rerank endpoints can carry bulk document corpora, so large-body POSTs are the egress signal. TLS-only. Monitored as a dev/data-platform destination.

services.name: Hugging Face (Hub + Inference + HuggingChat)
services.notes: Multi-modal risk: model/dataset uploads to the Hub (huggingface.co / cdn-lfs via git-LFS), serverless inference (api-inference.huggingface.co, router.huggingface.co), dedicated Inference Endpoints (*.endpoints.huggingface.cloud), and HuggingChat browser paste. The dataset/LFS push path is a real bulk-exfil vector (pushing a private dataset). TLS-only. Monitored; dataset/model pushes are the high-signal egress.

services.name: Poe (Quora multi-model aggregator)
services.notes: Aggregator front-end that proxies to many backend models (GPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.), so a single poe.com destination masks which model ultimately receives the data - a governance blind spot. Browser-paste/upload egress to poe.com; bot-API to api.poe.com. Dedicated hostname makes blocking easy but the aggregation hides the true recipient. Default unsanctioned.

services.name: Character.AI
services.notes: Consumer roleplay/chat companion; egress is conversational text pasted into character.ai. Limited enterprise value and known data-retention concerns make it a typical block candidate. neo.character.ai / plus.character.ai carry the chat traffic. TLS-only. Default unsanctioned, frequently escalated to blocked.

services.name: DeepSeek
services.notes: China-based provider; data residency/jurisdiction concerns lead many enterprises to block by default. Consumer egress via paste into chat.deepseek.com; developer egress to api.deepseek.com (OpenAI-compatible schema). Dedicated hostnames make SNI/host blocking reliable. TLS-only payloads. Default blocked in regulated environments; monitored at minimum elsewhere.

services.name: xAI Grok
services.notes: Consumer Grok is reachable at grok.com and embedded inside X (x.com / twitter.com), which complicates host-based control because blocking Grok-in-X means blocking the social platform. Developer egress to api.x.ai (OpenAI-compatible). Standalone grok.com paste is the cleanest detection point. TLS-only. Default unsanctioned.

services.name: Midjourney
services.notes: Image generation. Historically driven through Discord (so egress prompts/images ride discord.com / gateway.discord.gg - blocking Midjourney via Discord is impossible without blocking Discord entirely), now also a native web app at midjourney.com (alpha/web). Egress is text prompts and reference-image uploads; generated assets pull from cdn.midjourney.com. The Discord coupling is the key interception challenge. Default unsanctioned.

services.name: Replicate
services.notes: Model-hosting/inference marketplace; programmatic egress to api.replicate.com (run any community model). Inputs (images, audio, text, files) and outputs transit replicate.delivery / pbxt.replicate.delivery (CDN). Bulk media uploads to arbitrary third-party models are the egress concern. TLS-only. Monitored as a dev/inference destination.

services.name: Together AI
services.notes: Open-model inference and fine-tuning platform; programmatic egress to api.together.xyz (canonical) / api.together.ai. Fine-tuning file uploads are a bulk-exfil path (training-set upload). OpenAI-compatible schema. TLS-only. Monitored as a dev/inference and fine-tuning destination - flag large file uploads.

services.name: You.com
services.notes: AI search/assistant; browser-paste egress to you.com (YouChat/research modes) and developer egress to api.you.com / chat-api.you.com (Search and Research APIs). Dedicated hostnames make detection reliable. TLS-only. Default unsanctioned consumer assistant.

services.name: Local LLMs, gateways, and proxy/relay patterns
services.notes: Critical blind spots a host-allowlist misses: (1) Local inference - Ollama (127.0.0.1:11434), LM Studio, llama.cpp, vLLM - stays on loopback, never crosses a NetworkExtension content filter, so only Endpoint Security exec/file telemetry sees it; this is loopback-exfil into a local model that may then sync. (2) Aggregator/relay gateways - OpenRouter (openrouter.ai) and self-hosted LiteLLM proxy abstract the true model vendor behind one endpoint, defeating per-vendor host rules. (3) Tunnels - ngrok (*.ngrok.io / *.ngrok-free.app), Cloudflare Tunnel (*.trycloudflare.com) can relay prompts to an arbitrary backend, bypassing vendor-specific blocks. (4) Cloud-hosted variants - Azure OpenAI (*.openai.azure.com), AWS Bedrock (bedrock-runtime.<region>.amazonaws.com), Vertex - move LLM egress onto generic cloud hostnames. A DLP must treat these as escape hatches, not just enumerate name-brand vendors.
